verses.translation: Now, (we look into) the discipline of yoga.

verses.title: What is Yoga?
verses.translation: Yoga is restraining the modulations of the mind.
verses.comments: चित्त / citta, mind, also translates as consciousness

verses.translation: Then, (when the mind is restrained), (one) abides in the nature of the seer.
At other times, there is identification with the modulations of the mind.
The modulations are of five types, which could express themseleves as problematic or non-problematic.
verses.comments: for e.g. memory is a modulation and some memories could be painful while others are not.

verses.title: The Five Modulations
verses.translation: The five modulations are

verses.title: Pramāṇa - Proof / Logic
verses.translation: Pramāṇa (proof/logic) is sought in three ways

verses.title: Viparyaya - Wrong Knowledge
verses.translation: Viparyaya is incorrect understanding, when there is a gap between perception of the reality.

verses.title: Vikalpa - Imagination
verses.translation: Vikalpa stands for words (or ideas/thoughts) which have no corresponding objects. (Imagination, fantasies, baseless fears)

verses.title: Nidra - Sleep
verses.translation: Nidrā / sleep is when mind is void/empty (no content)

verses.title: Smriti - Memory
verses.translation: Memory is inability to let go of past experiences.
The modulations can be restrained by अभ्यास / abhyāsa (practice) and वैराग्य / vairāgya (dispassion).

verses.title: Abhyāsa - Practice
verses.translation: Abhyāsa (practice) is the effort to be established in yoga (or in the seer).
When practice is done over a long period of time, without a break and with a sense of respect, then you get firmly grounded in it.

verses.title: Vairāgya - Dispassion
verses.translation: Dispassion is having a say over the thirst that arises from sense objects, either experienced by oneself or heard from others.
Higher than that (dispassion) is the centeredness that comes from knowing the glory of the Self.
(Samādhi) with awareness can happen through logic, thoughts, bliss or sense of 'I am'
The mind comes to state of rest by the practices done earlier (in this or previous lives) and in this state, only residual impressions remain. This is another type of samādhi (without awareness)
For those who are immersed in nature or (are) without a body, the world is the content of the mind.
Other ways to obtain samādhi are through faith, valor, memory of your origin and awareness of a previous experience of samādhi.
Through intensity of practice, it (samādhi) is expedited.
Even among that (intensity of practice), it is qualified as mild, medium or intense.
Or (one can achieve samādhi) by surrendering to the Divine (īśvarā)
Divinity is that special consciousness which is untouched by misery (kleśa), action/impressions (karma), fruit of actions (vipākā) and desires (latent and manifest)
The seed of all knowingness abides in that (Lordship)
That (Lordship) is the Guru for those who came before too, as it is unobstructed by time.

verses.title: Om (ॐ)
verses.translation: That is addressed as Om (ॐ)
Its repetition and feeling its meaning.
From that (repetition of Om(ॐ) and feeling its meaning) is gained the knowledge of consciousness and the destruction of obstacles.

verses.title: The Nine Obstacles
verses.translation: The obstacles or distractions in the practice of Yoga are:
(The following) manifest along with the obstacles:
To counter these (obstacles), practise one principle. (एकतत्त्वभ्यासः / ekatattvabhyāsaḥ)
We attain a pleasant state of mind by cultivating feelings of
Or by breaking and elongating the flow of prāṇa (breath / life force)
Or by arresting (holding) the mind in that state which arises through contact with the sense objects.
Or by the sorrow-less state attained through the glowing intellect (ज्योतिष्मती / jyotiṣmatī)
Or by remembering the one who is devoid of craving.
Or through the knowledge of sleep and dreams.
Or by meditation according to one's liking.
One gains control over the smallest as well as the largest.

When the modulations of the mind are diminished, it becomes like a polished transparent crystal and alignment (samāpattiḥ) is established between the perceiver, perception and the object of perception.
That state when the gap between the word, its meaning and its experience, becomes insignificant, is called savitarkā samāpattiḥ - alignment with special logic.
When the memory (latent impressions) is purified, the "I" seems as if void and only "am" of the "I am" exists, it is called nirvitarkā samāpattiḥ.

verses.sanskrit: एतयैव सविचारा निर्विचारा च सूक्ष्मविषया व्याख्याता
verses.transliteration: etayaiva savicārā nirvicārā ca sūkṣmaviṣayā vyākhyātā
verses.translation: When the word-meaning-experience is about subtle objects, then savicārā and nirvicārā samāpatti are similarly explained
And the realm of these subtle objects extends all the way up to the Unmanifest.
All of these (mentioned above) are samādhi with a seed (sabījah samādhi).
Of these, when one attains proficiency in nirvicāra samādhi, that brings spiritual fulfillment.
In such a state, the consciousness is filled with awareness of truth (intuition)
The awareness (or knowledge) that comes from such a state is special compared to knowledge arising from the senses or the intellect because it is based on a different source (spiritual fulfillment)
The impressions born of such samādhi overrule the impressions generated from other experiences.
When such impressions (born of samādhi) are eliminated, then, everything having been restrained, seedless (nirbījah) samādhi results.
Endurance, self-study and surrender to the Divine, constitutes the yoga of action (Kriyā Yoga).
verses.comments: Kriyā Yoga is also part of Niyamās (2.32)

verses.translation: (The purpose of Kriyā Yoga is) to bring about samādhi and to minimize the afflictions or miseries.
The five miseries are:

verses.title: Avidyā - Ignorance (1)
verses.translation: Ignorance is the source of all other miseries. They can be in four stages: 1. dormant 2. weakened  3. occasional 4. very active.

verses.title: Avidyā - Ignorance (2)
verses.translation: Ignorance is mistaking the impermanent for the permanent, the impure for the pure, that which brings misery for that which brings happiness and non-Self for the Self.

verses.title: Asmitā - I-ness
verses.translation: When the seer is lost in the power of the scenery, a limited sense of "I" results.

verses.title: Rāga - Craving
verses.translation: Craving is the attraction to pleasure.

verses.title: Dveṣa - Aversion
verses.translation: Aversion is the repulsion from sorrow.

verses.title: Abhiniveśa - Fear
verses.translation: Even in the wise, there is an ever flowing, firmly established fear of loss or death.
When these (kleśās) are in subtle form, they can be dealt with by resolving them into their source.
Their modifications are destroyed by meditation.
These (five) miseries cause the stockpile of impressions (karmā) which has to be experienced in the current or future lives.
As long as those kleśās (miseries) remain as the root, they will keep producing three consequences - birth, span of life and experiences in that life.
These consequences (birth, lifespan and experiences) give joy or misery based on merit or demerit.

Since all these experiences lead to more consequences, anxiety and impressions, and the qualities of nature (guṇās) contradict each other, a wise, discriminating person, sees all wordily experiences as painful.
The misery which is yet to come is worth avoiding.
The cause of pain, which is to be avoided, is forgetting that one's Self is separate from the scenery.
The scenery exhibits three characteristics – luminosity, activity and inertia. It is of the nature of the five elements and the senses. The scenery is meant for experience and liberation.
There are four stages of the qualities 1. specific 2. unspecific 3. with an identity and 4. without a definite identity.
Though the seer is pure consciousness, it imagines the scenery (due to its intellect)
That (the seer) alone is the reason for the scenery to exist.
For the realized being, the scenery ceases to be, yet it exists for others.
The realization of the nature of the scenery and the seer is the purpose of their conjunction.
Ignorance is the cause of it (after conjunction of the scenery with the seer)

verses.sanskrit: तदभावात् संयोगाभावो हानं तद् दृशेः कैवल्यम्
verses.transliteration: tad-abhāvāt saṃyog-ābhāvo hānaṃ tad dṛśeḥ kaivalyam
verses.translation: Renunciation is the absence of the conjunction (between the seer and the scenery) resulting from the cessation of ignorance. That is the liberation of the seer.
Pure awareness of the distinction (discrimination) of the seer and the scenery is the means of renouncing (the scenery).
This discriminative awareness brings forth seven levels of intelligence.
Practice of the limbs of yoga eliminates impurities, brings the light of knowledge and discriminative awareness (awareness of the distinction between the seer and the scenery)

verses.title: The Eight Limbs
verses.translation: The eight limbs of Yoga are:

verses.title: Yamās
verses.translation: The Yamās are:
These great vows are not restricted by any consideration of the life-state, place, time or situation; they are universal.

verses.title: Niyamas
verses.translation: The Niyamās are:
verses.comments: The last three niyamās constitute Kriyā Yoga (2.1)

verses.translation: When negative thoughts obstruct the observance of yamās and niyamās, become aware of the consequences

verses.sanskrit: वितर्का हिंसादयः कृतकारितानुमोदिता लोभक्रोधमोहपूर्वका मृदुमध्याधिमात्रा दुःखाज्ञानानन्तफला इति प्रतिपक्षभावनम्
verses.transliteration: vitarkā hiṃsādayaḥ kṛta-kārit-ānumoditā lobha-krodha-moha-pūrvakā mṛdu-madhy-ādhimātrā duḥkh-ājñān-ānanta-phalā- iti pratipakṣa-bhāvanam
verses.translation: Negative thoughts like violence etc. (opposite to the yamās and niyamās) lead to actions performed directly by oneself, caused to be done to others, or approved of when done by others. All these may be preceded by or performed through anger, greed or delusion. They can be mild, moderate or intense in nature. Remind oneself that these negative thoughts and actions are the cause of unending misery and ignorance. This is (meant by) the awareness of the consequences.
Being established in non-violence (ahimsā) causes everyone in the vicinity to lose the feeling of hostility.
Being established in Truth (satya), causes one's action to bear fruit.
Being established in non-stealing (asteya) causes all the jewels to present themselves.
Being established in chastity (brahmacarya) gives great strength.
Being established in non-accumulation (aparigraha) gives complete knowledge of the underlying causes and reasons of previous lifetimes.
Through cleanliness and purity (śauca), one develops an attitude of disinterest towards one's own body, becoming disinclined towards contact with the bodies of others.
And purification of the intellect, pleasantness in the mind, one-pointedness, victory over the senses and the eligibility for self-realization.
From contentment (santoṣa), comes unparalleled happiness.
Penance (tapaḥ) eliminates impurities from the body and brings perfection to the senses.
From self-study and reflection (svādhyāya), one attains contact with the subtle beings.
By surrendering to the Divine (īśvara praṇidhānā), perfection in samādhi is attained.
Posture is that which is steady and comfortable.
By letting go of the effort, alignment with infinity is attained.
Through that, one is able to be unperturbed by the opposites.
Having done that (while in the āsana), interrupting the flow of inhalation and exhalation is called prānāyāma.

The various aspects of prānāyāma are exhalation, inhalation and suspension of breath. The other parameters to be looked into are the space (of the movement of breath), the time and count while the breath is deep and subtle.
The fourth type (of prānāyāma) happens when the (effort of) exhalation and inhalation is given up.
Thereby, the veil over the inner light is thinned.
And the ability to focus one's mind follows.

sections.title: Fifth Limb: Pratyāhāra / Withdrawal of the senses[54-55]

verses.translation: Withdrawal of the senses (pratyāhāra) is when, as a result of not coming in touch with sense objects, the senses (turn inwards and) follow the nature of the mind.
This gives supreme control over the senses.
Holding the mind in a place is focus (dhāraṇā)
There, an unbroken flow in the content of the mind is meditation (dhyāna)
That (meditation) itself becomes samādhi when only pure awareness remains, almost as if there is no self-cognition.
When all the three - dhāraṇā, dhyāna and samādhi - come together, it is samyamaḥ.
Mastery of that (samyama) brings transcendental awareness.
That (transcendental awareness) should be applied in previous planes of awareness.
These three (dhāraṇā, dhyāna and samādhi) are subtler (internal) than the previous ones (Yamā, Niyamā, Āsana, Prāṇāyāma and Pratyahāra)
However, they (dhāraṇā, dhyāna and samādhi) are gross (external) as compared to nirbīja (seedless) samādhi.

verses.title: 1. Nirodha Pariṇāma
verses.translation: When the tendency of the mind to express is diminishing and its tendency of restraint is prevailing, then the conjunction of the mind with the restraining tendency is Nirodha Pariṇāma.
A steady flow of such a state results from this impression (due to the practice of Nirodha Pariṇāma)

verses.title: 2. Samādhi Pariṇāma
verses.translation: When the tendency of the mind to be scattered diminishes and its tendency to be one pointed rises, that is samādhi pariṇāma.

verses.title: 3. Ekāgratā Pariṇāma
verses.translation: When the one-pointedness of the mind rises and subsides again like any other thought, that is Ekāgratā pariṇāma of the mind.

By this (the three pariṇāmas above), the transformation in the essential nature, the transformation due to time and the transformation of characteristics in the senses (indriyās) and the (five) elements are explained.
The dharmī is the unchanging basis common to the latent, manifest and unmanifest characteristics.
The types of transformation differ due to the differences in the sequence of characteristics.
By samyama on the three-fold transformation (of essential nature, due to time and of characteristics), there comes knowledge of the past and future.

The name associated with an object, the meaning of the name and the object itself are commingled with one another. By samyama on the distinction between these three, comes the knowledge of the sounds made by all beings.
Through the direct perception of latent impressions, comes the knowledge of previous births.
By samyama on the content of one's mind comes the knowledge of others' mind.
The underlying support of that knowledge (of the other person's mind) remains unperceived because that is not the subject of samyama.
By samyama on the form of one's own physical body, the perceivability of the body is suspended and it becomes invisible to other people.
verses.comments: There is one more śhloka which says that, similarly, other senses can also be suspended. In the same way, as described in relation to sight, one is able to suspend the ability of the body to be heard, touched, tasted or smelt.

verses.sanskrit: सोपक्रमं निरुपक्रमं च कर्म तत्संयमादपरान्तज्ञानमरिष्टेभ्यो वा
verses.transliteration: sopakramaṃ nirupakramaṃ ca karma tat-saṃyamād-aparānta-jñānam-ariṣṭebhyo vā
verses.translation: Karma is of two kinds - either fast or slow to manifest. By samyama on these karmās or on the signs of death, comes the knowledge of the time of death.
By samyama on friendliness and other attitudes, there comes great strength.
verses.comments: Reference to maitri, karuṇā, muditā and upekshā (1.33)

verses.translation: By samyama on the strength of an elephant comes a similar strength.
By shining the inner light, comes the knowledge of subtle, hidden and distant objects.
By samyama on the Sun (the right/sūrya nādi) comes the knowledge of the physical universe.
By samyama on the Moon (the left/chandra nāḍi) comes the knowledge of the arrangement of the stars.
By samyama on the Pole Star (Dhruva) comes the knowledge of the movement of the stars.
By samyama on the navel center, comes the knowledge of the arrangement of the systems of the body.
By samyama on the pit of the throat (kaṇṭhakūpe), thirst and hunger are disappear.
By samyama on the central channel below the throat (kūrma nāḍi), comes stability.
By doing samyama on the coronal light (top of the head), visions of the masters (siddhās) can come.
Or through intuition, everything could be known.
By samyama on the heart, comes the knowledge of the consciousness.

The subtlest aspect of the intellect (sattva) and the Self (puruṣa) are quite different. When they commingle (and one starts identifying with the intellect), it results in experiences which are really for the sake of another (the Self). By samyama on pure consciousness (which is distinct from the subtlest aspect of intellect), comes the knowledge of the Self.
From that (knowledge of the Self) arises intuitive hearing, touch, vision, taste and smell.
These (heightened intuitive abilities / siddhis) are obstacles to samādhi but appear to be attainments or powers to the mind turned outwards.
By loosening the causes of bondage and by the knowledge of the passages of the mind, there comes the ability to enter another body.
The mastery over udāna, the upward flowing prāṇa-vāyu, brings the ability to remain untouched by mud, water, thorns and the like, as well as the ability to levitate.
The mastery over samāna, the prāṇa flowing in the navel area, brings radiance or fire.
Samyama on the relationship between space and the sense of hearing brings about a heightened sense of hearing.

verses.title: The Flying Sutra
verses.translation: By samyama on the relationship between body and space, the lightness of cotton is attained and with that comes the ability to move through space.
When the mind can extend beyond the body effortlessly, it is the state of a great disincarnate one. With that, the veil over the light of knowledge is diminished.
By samyama on the aspects of the elements (bhūtās) - gross form, essence, subtle form, interconnectedness and purposefulness, mastery over the elements follows.
Through that (mastery over the elements), come powers such making the body small. Perfection of the body is attained as well and the elements cause it no harm.
verses.comments: Aṇimā-ādi refers to the ashta (eight) siddhis -> Aṇimā, Mahimā, Garimā, Laghimā, Prāpti, Prākāmya, Īśitva and Vaśitva

verses.translation: Perfection of the body includes beauty, gracefulness, strength and unyielding hardness in receiving blows when struck.
By samyama on the process of perception by senses, their essence, I-ness, connectedness and purposefulness, comes mastery over the senses.
By that (mastery over the senses) comes sharpness of mind, perception beyond the senses and supremacy over the Primal Cause (the cause of all existence).
Upon being established in the knowledge of the distinction between the intellect (sattva) and pure consciousness (purūsha), one attains mastery over all kinds of feelings and also omniscience.
The desirelessness for even that (the attainments in the previous sūtras) destroys the seeds of bondage and one attains absolute liberation (kaivalya).
When invited by the celestial beings, there is no reason to accept the offer or smile with pride as it may again lead to undesirable events.
Samyama on the present moment and its flow results in knowledge that is born from discrimination.
This knowledge (born from discrimination) brings awareness of the difference between two similar objects, which are not distinguishable by type (or category), qualities or location.

verses.sanskrit: तारकं सर्वविषयं सर्वथाविषयम् अक्रमं चेति विवेकजं ज्ञानम्
verses.transliteration: tārakaṃ sarva-viṣayaṃ sarvathā-viṣayam akramaṃ ce-ti vivekajaṃ jñānam
verses.translation: This knowledge born from discrimination is intuitive and applies to everything, by all means, and arises spontaneously (unlike knowledge born of logic which follows a sequence of inferences).
When the intellect and the consciousness become equally pure, that is absolute liberation (kaivalya).
The different kinds of perfection (siddhis) come with birth or are attained through herbs, mantra, penance or samādhi.
Transition to another life-state or form (in the next birth) comes as a result of what is filled in one's nature (forms natural tendency)

Incidental cause or action does not fill up one's nature (which happens on its own) but it does remove the obstacles to the process, like a farmer (removes the barriers to the field and water fills it up on its own).
Doership comes solely from a sense of I-ness (asmitā).
It is the one mind that is the director or different activities (or tendencies)
There, the tendency or activity born of meditation is free from latent impressions.
The actions of a yogi are neither white nor black. They are three-fold for others.
As they (the three-fold actions) lead up to fruition, they manifest corresponding latent impressions.
Even if there is interruption in life-state, time and space, karmā unfolds with unbroken continuity because memory and latent impressions are of the same nature.
Those (patterns of impressions) are beginningless because the desire to live is eternal.
verses.comments: As long as there is a will to live, some or other patterns keep happening in you and you get into those patterns

verses.translation: Impressions are held together by cause, motive, basis and support. Disappearance of these four factors causes the impressions to disappear as well.
The past and the future exist in reality, owing to differences in characteristics.
Manifest (when in the present) or subtle (not manifested when in the past or the future), they (characteristics) are of the nature of the (three) guṇās (sattva, rajas and tamas).
The reality of an object comes from the uniformity in change (of the guṇās).
Some objects may be perceived differently by different minds because of the difference in paths of perception.

verses.transliteration: na ca-ika-citta-tantraṃ‌ vastu tad-apramāṇakaṃ‌ tadā‌ kiṃ‌ syāt
verses.translation: The (existence of the) object does not depend on any one mind. If it does, what happens to the object when it is not perceived by that mind?
The object needs to influence or color the mind and accordingly it is known or unknown (by the mind).
The modifications of the mind are always known by its lord (the puruṣa) as the puruṣa is changeless.
That (mind) is not self-illuminating as it an object of perception (by the puruṣa).
And, both (the mind and the puruṣa) cannot be cognized simultaneously.
If one mind is known by another, which is known by another and so on, there would be an endless progression and mixture of memories.
Consciousness is unchanging and when the intellect assumes its form, self-cognition is possible.

Though the mind-field is full of innumerable impressions, it can act only in combination (with the senses, sense objects, ego etc). Therefore, its objective is for another (not for its own sake; here 'another' is the puruṣa, the witnessing consciousness).
For one who distinctly experiences the Self (distinct from the intellect), the curiosity about the nature of one's own Self vanishes.
Then, the mind starts inclining towards discrimination and gravitates towards absolute liberation.
When there are gaps or breaks in that discrimination, other thoughts and ideas come up in the mind due to earlier impressions.
These can be removed with the means of getting rid of misery, explained earlier.
When there is no interest even in the fruits of being established in that discriminative awareness, there comes the samādhi which brings a cloud of virtues.
Thereafter, all afflictions and impressions are removed
Then, free from all the veils of impurities, with the knowledge that is infinite, there is almost nothing to be known.
Thereafter, the purpose of the (three) guṇās is fulfilled and their sequence of transformation comes to an end.
Sequence (in the above sutra) means that which is perceivable at the end of the transformation that happens through moments of time.

verses.transliteration: puruṣārtha-śūnyānāṃ guṇānāṃ pratiprasavaḥ‌ kaivalyaṃ svarūpa-pratiṣṭhā‌ vā‌ citi-śaktir-iti
verses.translation: Absolute liberation is when the guṇās, now devoid of the puruṣa's purpose (having attained it already), resolve back to their source or when the consciousness is established in its nature.
